data.title: Deaths in Indonesia’s flood-hit Sumatra rise to 72 – ‘we’ve lost everything’
data.description:
Heavy monsoon rains have pummelled Indonesia’s Sumatra island over the past week, causing widespread flooding and landslides, and leaving at least 72 dead with hundreds more missing.
Villagers and residential areas were swept away or buried by mudslides, affecting more than 2,000 homes in North Sumatra, according to Indonesia’s Disaster Management Agency.
Some 5,000 people across the province had taken refuge in government shelters, the agency added.
On Friday, the agency confirmed 57 deaths in North Sumatra. Six people were also found dead in neighbouring Aceh province, where 11 remained missing, while West Sumatra reported nine fatalities.
In the city of Medan, the provincial capital of North Sumatra, floodwaters began to rise in the early hours of Thursday morning, bringing the city to a standstill and destroying homes and businesses.
Standing outside the remains of his home in Medan’s Helvetia district, 18-year-old student Ferdika told This Week in Asia that his family was woken around 4am (5am Hong Kong time) on Thursday as water surged into their bedrooms.
“I grabbed my two younger brothers, who are 6 and 9, and we all rushed outside,” he said. “I went back to try and get some clothes and other possessions, but within an hour, the water had risen to my neck.”

Ferdika fled with his mother, younger siblings and older brother and sister to a makeshift government camp close to his home, where they slept overnight. He and his older brother returned to their home on Friday morning to find that the waters had subsided, but all their possessions were destroyed.
“Our fridge was filled with water and mud, and all our plates and cutlery had floated away,” Ferdika said.
Throughout Friday afternoon, he worked to move the remains of their possessions outside, hoping that they would dry out, including clothes, shoes, mattresses and a wooden cross decorated with tinsel ahead of the Christmas holiday. The remnants of a sewing machine, used by Ferdika’s mother to mend and sew clothes for the family, also sat among the debris.
Ferdika said that he did not know how the family would recover, as many of their possessions were caked in mud and could not be cleaned. He hoped that the government would provide financial help to affected families.
While rescue efforts across Sumatra are under way, lack of access to remote areas due to unstable ground and impassable roads has hampered rescuers.

Telecommunications also remain down across much of the region, impeding the search for survivors and making it challenging for the government to assess the number of people affected.
“With many missing and some remote areas still unreachable, the death toll is likely to rise,” police spokesperson for North Sumatra, Ferry Walintukan, said in a statement on Thursday.
Reni Anggreni’s salon in Medan, which provides hair services as well as make-up and spa treatments, was among the businesses destroyed by the flood.
“Everything is gone,” the business owner told This Week in Asia. “The waters started to flood my salon at around 9am on Thursday and rose up to my waist.”
Anggreni took refuge on the second floor of the salon, but was not able to save most of her equipment, including electronic items such as hairdryers, and spa equipment like steam machines.
“My fridge also exploded as I forgot to unplug it as I rushed upstairs,” she said.
However, Anggreni said that she was trying to be stoic, despite the loss of her business and that she did not have flood insurance.

“The most important thing is that I was not hurt and can still work. I hope God will hear my prayers and help me to replace all my equipment so that I can have lots of customers again.”
At a neighbouring salon, owner Maye Siahaan said she was in shock, with her business ruined by water and thick mud.
“I have just been sitting in a chair since it happened, wondering what I am going to do,” she told This Week in Asia.
Siahaan said that water had started to overflow from a drain in front of her salon on Thursday morning. Along with her two staff, Angel Silalahi and Mei Pangaribuan, they grabbed equipment such as hair straighteners and dryers, and rushed to the upper floor of the salon.
Silalahi told This Week in Asia: “We could only watch as everything floated away. It went in all different directions. We couldn’t save everything like our fridge, sofa and chairs.”
All three women said they had moved to Medan for work from the town of Kutacane in Indonesia’s Aceh province, which has also been inundated with rain and widespread flooding.

None of the women had been able to contact their parents and families in Kutacane - around a five-hour drive from Medan – since Wednesday morning, as telephone and internet networks across Aceh were down.
“We haven’t been able to speak to anyone to ask if they are safe, and we heard that there is no electricity in Aceh now,” Silalahi said.
According to Aceh’s Disaster Mitigation Agency, some 47,000 people have been displaced across the province, and over 1,500 have been forced to take refuge in government shelters, with elderly and other residents sheltering in local mosques.
In Medan, salon owner Siahaan said she was especially upset with the timing of the flooding so close to the holidays, traditionally a time when the business booms with patrons requesting make-up and hairstyling services to attend Christmas parties and end-of-year events.
“It is about to be Christmas, our families in Aceh are missing, and we have lost everything,” she said.

data.title: China Vanke hit with fresh S&P downgrade amid mounting default concerns
data.description:
Global ratings agency S&P has downgraded China Vanke, as the troubled developer grapples with mounting debt and fragile cash flow, raising concerns about a renewed liquidity crisis across the country’s real estate industry.
S&P on Friday cut Vanke’s long-term issuer rating to CCC- from CCC and likewise lowered the long-term issue rating on senior unsecured notes of its Hong Kong subsidiary, marking the second downgrade for the developer in less than a month.
All ratings were placed on CreditWatch with negative implications, it said.
The decision reflected Vanke’s “unsustainable” financial commitments, S&P said, citing weak liquidity and a looming bond maturity of about 11.4 billion yuan (US$1.6 billion) between December 2025 and May 2026.
“Over the next six months, the company faces a sizeable bond maturity wall while operating cash flow will be negative, and its accessible cash buffer will likely be thin,” the ratings firm said.
“The company’s debt obligations are currently vulnerable to risks of non-payment or distressed restructuring,” it added.
The downgrade followed a media report earlier this week saying the Shenzhen-based developer could be asked to adopt more “market-oriented” measures to handle its debt, implying that the firm may face a restructuring.
On Wednesday night, Vanke proposed extending repayment for a 2 billion yuan onshore bond – its first such move. A bondholder meeting is due to be held on December 10.

Backed by Shenzhen Metro Group, Vanke had long been viewed as one of China’s more financially stable developers and was widely seen as a bellwether for the country’s struggling property sector.
A dozen developers, including industry leader Country Garden Holdings and China Evergrande Group, have defaulted since 2021 when Beijing imposed the “three red lines” policy to impose lending caps and curb leverage.
Evergrande was ordered into liquidation in January 2024.
Vanke’s potential restructuring has added to fears of a new wave of credit defaults that could deal a further blow to China’s property sector. Its shares in Shenzhen fell 1.7 per cent to a fresh nine-year low on Friday, while the Hong Kong-listed stock staged a slight rebound after steep losses earlier in the week.
Shenzhen Metro Group, Vanke’s largest shareholder, has injected liquidity support including some 30 billion yuan in loans to the developer this year, according to an exchange filing earlier this month.
Even so, Vanke has 364.3 billion yuan in interest-bearing liabilities, and it reported a loss of 16.1 billion yuan in the three months ended September 30, roughly doubling its losses from a year earlier.

data.title: ICC rejects Philippines ex-president Duterte’s request for release
data.description: Rodrigo Duterte, the former president of the Philippines, will remain in detention in The Hague, appeals judges at the International Criminal Court ruled on Friday, dismissing defence arguments the 80-year-old should be released due to his advanced age and alleged declining health.\nMore to follow …\n

data.title: Indonesian durian sellers smell big opportunity in China, world’s largest market
data.description:
Indonesia is looking to take a bite out of the lucrative durian trade to China following stronger bilateral cooperation in the sector, according to the Indonesian Durian Plantation Association.
Since Indonesia signed a frozen durian export protocol with China’s General Administration of Customs last May, eight local companies have passed the verification process, according to an Antara news agency report on Wednesday.
This means that Indonesian producers can export directly to China, bypassing intermediaries such as Thailand and Malaysia.
Last year, Indonesia exported 600 tonnes of durian, valued at US$1.8 million, mainly to Thailand and Hong Kong, according to Statistics Indonesia. This figure was the highest in the last five years.


The country has been trying to tap into China’s increasing appetite for the king of fruits but has been lagging behind durian producers Thailand and Vietnam due to logistics and licensing, according to Eliza Mardian, a researcher at the Centre for Reform on Economics Indonesia.
China is the world’s largest durian consumer, accounting for 91 per cent of global consumption. It imported 1.56 million tonnes last year, totalling nearly US$7 billion.
Noting that licensing was no longer a problem after the frozen durian protocol, Mardian urged Jakarta to help farmers with the logistics, such as building infrastructure for freezing and storing the fresh fruits.
Indonesia’s major durian-growing regions are in Java, Sumatra, Kalimantan and Sulawesi.
In anticipation of higher demand, farmers in Parigi Moutong, a major durian-producing regency in central Sulawesi, have planted more than 110,000 trees on 1,100 hectares (2,700 acres) of land.
Parigi Moutong, which reportedly has 16 China-approved durian packing houses, aims to earn up to 1 trillion rupiah (US$60 million) via exports in the first quarter of next year.
Other durian-producing countries are also eager to meet the growing demand for the thorny treat.
On November 10, Malaysia’s Durian Manufacturers Association (DMA) asked the agriculture and food security ministry to recognise durian as the national fruit and to declare July 7 as National Durian Day, according to The Star newspaper.

This raised heckles in Indonesia, with its Food Affairs Coordinating Minister Zulkifli Hasan saying his country had a stronger claim as it produced more of the fruit than Malaysia.
“Indonesia produced nearly 2 million tonnes of durian in 2024. This figure is far higher compared to Malaysia. With this fact, I believe durian is Indonesia’s national fruit,” he said, as quoted by MSN news portal.
“If we talk about national symbols, they must be based on data and reality. Nusantara durian is our strength in Asia. According to research data, Indonesia possesses 21 out of the 27 known durian species in the world, and as of 2024, a total of 114 superior varieties [in the country] have been registered.”
DMA president Eric Chan said its proposal was about celebrating heritage, not asserting ownership.
“Both Malaysia and Indonesia share deep cultural and historical ties with durian,” he told The Star. “Making it Malaysia’s national fruit does not diminish Indonesia’s right to do the same.”
Although Malaysia’s total production is lower, its durian exports command a higher price because of premium home-grown varieties such as Musang King, Black Thorn and D24.
The country ships the fruit to over 40 markets, including China, South Korea, the UK and the UAE, according to the Bernama news agency.
Its durian exports totalled 1.18 billion ringgit (US$285 million) last year, and the Ministry of Agriculture and Food Security has projected that this could rise to 1.8 billion ringgit by 2030.

data.title: Li Minyong, noted Chinese chemist and Royal Society fellow, dies at age 49
data.description:
Li Minyong, a prominent medicinal chemist and a fellow of multiple international research societies, died on November 16 at the age of 49.
His sudden death marks China’s latest loss of a leading scientist at the height of their research career.
Li, a professor and deputy dean of the school of pharmaceutical sciences at Hainan University, dedicated himself to pioneering drug discovery through light-controlled and bioactive visualisation technologies.
He was elected a Fellow of Britain’s Royal Society of Chemistry in 2019 and of the Royal Society of Biology in 2021. In 2022, he was elected a Fellow of the International Association of Advanced Materials in Sweden.
Last year, he was named a Changjiang Distinguished Professor, one of the most prestigious academic titles in China’s higher education granted by the Ministry of Education.
Li broke through multiple critical core technologies for visualising target proteins in major diseases, making significant contributions to biomedical innovation in China, according to an obituary released by his institution on the evening of November 17.
“His scholarly approach was steadfast and rigorous, yielding outstanding academic achievements,” the obituary said.
Li is also remembered for his contributions to talent development. “His exemplary conduct as an educator, who willingly served as a stepping stone for others, earned him deep affection from both students and colleagues,” the obituary said.
“The sudden passing of Professor Li is a significant loss to China’s educational and pharmaceutical communities.”
Tributes poured in from his students, friends and fellow researchers, most of whom described him as a respected mentor who was “rigorous in his scholarship, profoundly knowledgeable, and always wearing a smile in class”.
According to a report by the news website Shanghai Observer, Li was attending an academic conference in the southern city of Guangzhou from November 14 to 16.
On the last day, immediately after the event concluded, he became ill and collapsed outside the hotel. He was rushed to hospital but died despite the best efforts of medical staff.
Li’s death marks the latest premature loss for China’s science fraternity. Recent Chinese media and academic reports have documented the deaths of multiple scientists in their 30s and 40s, with commentary linking some cases to overwork, stress and the high-stakes research culture in the country.


Also on November 16, former University of California, Berkeley, scientist and biomedical researcher Xie Hongxue, 44, died of an unnamed illness, according to Shanghai-based news site ThePaper.cn. He joined West Anhui University’s materials science and engineering school as a lecturer about five years ago.
Li joined China Pharmaceutical University in eastern China as an undergraduate student in 1995, and he obtained his PhD there in 2005.
He then moved to the United States for postdoctoral training at Georgia State University, where he served as an assistant professor from 2007 to 2009.
In 2009, he returned to China to join the school of pharmaceutical sciences at Shandong University, before joining Hainan University in June.
His principal areas of research included the development of innovative visualisation-based drug and probe molecules, photopharmacology – the use of light-activated or light-switchable molecules to precisely control the activity of biological systems – and chemical biology studies.
He led several major projects funded by the National Natural Science Foundation of China – the country’s largest funder of basic research – including a project on visualisation research into fluorescent probes for adrenergic receptors.
According to his university profile page, he published more than 200 Science Citation-indexed papers and holds 22 invention patents. He also served as editor-in-chief of the prestigious journal Medicinal Research Reviews, and sat on the editorial boards of several academic journals, including Protein and Peptide Letters.
As deputy dean of the pharmaceutical sciences school, Li was also responsible for research, international collaboration and research-to-market translation.

data.title: Thailand flood death toll doubles as government faces mounting criticism
data.description:
The death toll from flooding and landslides in Thailand climbed steeply on Friday, heaping misery on a country struggling with a deep economic slump and triggering criticism of the nation’s roughly 10-week-old government.
There have been 145 fatalities across southern Thai provinces, with 110 deaths in Songkhla province alone, government spokesman Siripong Angkasakulkiat said in a briefing in Bangkok. That’s almost double the toll reported early on Friday. More than 1.25 million households and 3.6 million people have been affected in some way, the Department of Disaster Prevention and Mitigation said earlier.
Songkhla province recorded a sharp increase in the death toll after flooding began to subside. News reports showed rescuers gained more access to residential areas that had previously been submerged under high water and recovered more bodies, particularly in Hat Yai, the largest city in the south.
The disaster department reported on Friday morning that waters have receded in most of the affected areas, but levels remain high in some locations. The Meteorological Department said rainfall has decreased in the south but warned of thunderstorms in some areas.
The flooding caused severe disruption, leaving thousands of people stranded, rendering streets impassable and submerging low-rise buildings and vehicles.

Videos and photos from the affected areas on Friday show damaged roads, fallen power poles, household appliances and debris washed away by floodwaters piled along the streets. Abandoned cars were overturned or stacked atop one another, apparently swept away by powerful currents.
The flooding comes at the end of a difficult year for Thailand, which in March was severely rocked by an earthquake in neighbouring Myanmar, and in July saw border clashes with Cambodia that killed dozens. The economy shrank 0.6 per cent in the three months through September from the previous quarter and tourism has been sputtering, and the floods will add to economic challenges, especially in the south.
“The government can’t deny its responsibility,” Siripong told reporters, acknowledging that “errors have occurred” during efforts to manage the disaster.
There has been criticism of the government’s handling of the floods, though Thai Prime Minister Anutin Charnvirakul, who is heading for an election next year, earlier this week invoked special emergency laws to accelerate relief efforts.
The minister overseeing the emergency flood operations centre, Paradon Prissanananthakul, walked out of a Thursday press briefing after being asked if the government should admit misjudging the situation, leading to deaths.
“A big clean-up will be implemented as the water level is receding,” Paradorn said at Friday’s press conference.
The past week has seen parts of Southeast Asia inundated with heavy rains, with Malaysia, the Philippines and Vietnam also affected.


In Indonesia, tropical cyclone Senyar’s heavy rains triggered flash floods and landslides in three provinces on Sumatra island, the nation’s main oil palm growing region. At least 72 people have died over the past few days, with dozens more missing and hundreds of families displaced, according to the National Disaster Mitigation Agency.
While the Thai toll has climbed quickly, monsoon rains in the country’s lower southern region are forecast to ease over the next day, though thunderstorms are still expected, according to the Meteorological Department. There should be little rain from November 30 to December 4, it said.
Bank of Thailand Senior Director Pranee Sutthasri said the floods have already disrupted economic activity and that the provinces of Songkhla and Nakhon Sri Thammarat, among the worst affected, account for about 2.6 per cent of annual gross domestic product.
Cross-border tourism has also been hit, with roughly 10,000 Malaysian visitors entering through southern provinces each day, she said.

She said the overall economic impact will partly depend on post-flood reconstruction spending, adding that the central bank will assess the situation at its next interest rate meeting on December 17. Some analysts say the magnitude of the damage could raise the odds of a Bank of Thailand rate cut at its December 17 meeting.
Krungsri Research has estimated economic losses from the floods in Thailand at up to 23.6 billion baht (US$734 million), with the hotel and restaurant sectors suffering “significant” damage. Separately, the University of the Thai Chamber of Commerce put preliminary losses at as much as 1.5 billion baht a day.
Losses to the industrial sector in the affected provinces will still have to be assessed further, according to Supakit Boonsiri, director general of the Office of Industrial Economics. There are about 3,500 industrial manufacturers located across nine southern provinces, Supakit said.

data.title: Chinese homeless man fakes wealth, hires women for companionship, lives luxury life
data.description:
A homeless Chinese man deceived others by posing as a wealthy individual and, over a span of 10 days, managed to “hire” five women for companionship, using their money to fund visits to high-end establishments, buy new clothes, and even acquire the latest smartphone.
The scam came to light in August when police in Putuo District, Shanghai, received a report from a woman using the alias Liu Qian.
She claimed that she had been swindled by a 36-year-old man surnamed Chen, who pretended to be a nightclub owner.
Liu recounted that, the day before, she had acted as a local guide, accompanying Chen to dinner and later to various entertainment venues. He had promised to pay her 3,500 yuan (US$500) in cash.

The following day, Chen arrived at Liu’s home carrying a black plastic bag, which he opened to reveal around 50,000 to 60,000 yuan (US$7,000 – US$8,500) in cash.
He claimed that, for feng shui reasons, the money needed to be placed under her bed.
Chen stated that he wanted to continue hiring Liu, asserting the cash would be sufficient to compensate her and cover their upcoming expenses.
Coincidentally, Liu needed to pay rent that day and intended to use part of the money.
However, after Chen stepped out to “take a call,” she opened the bag and discovered it was filled with training banknotes typically used by bank tellers for practice.
Upon investigation, police determined that Chen frequently visited high-end venues, each time accompanied by a different woman. He made them cover all expenses, including food, drinks, hotels, and taxis.
The women willingly paid, believing Chen was affluent after seeing a large stack of what appeared to be real cash. By the time they realised it was a fraud, Chen had already vanished.
When he was finally arrested, even his clothing and the latest smartphone he owned were paid for by these women.

To execute the scam, Chen also carefully used a secondary account to pose as an agent in chat groups, posting “high-paying companionship” job offers.
During in-person meetings, he displayed stacks of training banknotes, strategically sandwiching a few real bills on the top and bottom. He then placed the cash in a fixed location and promised to pay the full amount afterwards.
When he failed to find a new victim quickly, he soon fell into financial distress. On such nights, he would sleep on benches in public parks, waiting until morning to scout for his next target.

Investigations have revealed that Chen defrauded five women. He has been formally arrested on charges of fraud.
His scam, reported by Shanghai Media Group, has ignited widespread discussion on Chinese social media.
One person quipped: “With enough guts, anything is possible.”
Another remarked: “Money isn’t for women to spend. It’s for them to see. This guy really maxed out the meaning of that saying.”

data.title: Hong Kong football bosses expected to postpone matches, clubs help fire relief effort
data.description:
Hong Kong’s sporting community has continued to offer its support in the wake of the growing tragedy in Tai Po, where a neighbourhood fire has so far claimed more than 100 lives.
Groups have begun organising collections of essential items for those affected by the blaze, which ripped through several residential buildings in Wang Fuk Court on Wednesday and Thursday, leaving thousands homeless.
And in the wake of the deadliest fire in the city in the past 30 years, Hong Kong’s biggest off-road race, the Oxfam Trailwalker, the annual cyclothon and all men’s and women’s rugby fixtures have been called off, while the Football Association of Hong Kong, China was expected to announce on Friday that all games this weekend had been postponed.
The Hong Kong, China Squash Association (HKCSA), meanwhile, said that “as a gesture of deep concern for those affected”, they would “adjust certain activities” for the Milwaukee Hong Kong Squash Open, which begins on Monday.
In a statement, the association said that “as a mark of respect and mourning for the incident” it had cancelled both a welcome reception planned for the eve of the tournament and a pre-finals performance on December 7.


But, it has opted to continue the event - a platinum tournament on the Professional Squash Association (PSA) World Tour - after “thorough consideration and discussion” with the PSA.
“[The tournament] involves numerous overseas players who have already arrived in Hong Kong and entails complex international scheduling,” read the HKCSA statement, which added that “the association has decided to proceed with the main event to ensure the tournament’s integrity and fulfil its commitment to all participating players”.
The Sports Federation & Olympic Committee of Hong Kong, China (SF&OC) called off a press conference scheduled for Monday to announce details for the Cathay 2025 Hong Kong Sports Stars Awards.
“SF&OC extends its deepest condolences to those who lost their lives, and sends its heartfelt sympathies to all affected by the tragedy,” read a statement. “We stand in solidarity with the community, and hope that everyone will come together to support those in need as we walk through this difficult time side by side.”
Separately, the University Sports Federation of Hong Kong, China announced it had cancelled all sport scheduled for this weekend.
Not everything has stopped though, and despite the cancellation of this year’s Oxfam Trailwalker event, sources tell the Post a “great number” of teams set out to walk from Pak Tam Chung on Friday morning.
“A lot of people are of course disappointed by the cancellation, but they walk regardless, same as in 2019,” the source, a former Oxfam staff member, says.
“My view is that it should not be cancelled but rather turned into a memorial walk with all funds raised going to the tragedy.”
With the weekend now free of sport, clubs have started organising donation drives, and Kowloon Rugby Club will hold theirs at King’s Park on Saturday from 1pm to 4pm.
“All of us at Kowloon Rugby Club extend our condolences to everyone impacted by the tragedy in Tai Po,” David Scott, the club’s chairman, said.
“We join the rest of the rugby community and the people of Hong Kong in mourning the victims and offering our thoughts and support to their families and others affected.”
A statement from Hong Kong Scottish thanked “all the firefighters, medical professionals, emergency response teams and every hero who is standing strong during the … tragedy”.

data.title: Why Franz Ferdinand’s Alex Kapranos loves Hong Kong gigs, plus insight on latest album
data.description:
Universal fears are the major recurring theme in Scottish art rock band Franz Ferdinand’s 2025 album The Human Fear. But there is nothing fearful about the group’s musical approach as the members get deeply personal and bravely vulnerable.
“‘Hooked’ starts off with the line, ‘I got the fear, got the human fear, that’s all right’,” says Alex Kapranos, the band’s lead vocalist, lead guitarist and primary songwriter, of the track that was inspired by his own experience with parenthood in 2023.
“The song is about facing up to those existential fears that we all have and the things that allow us to cope with them.
“I was talking about becoming a father and how those existential fears didn’t disappear, but they seemed irrelevant when compared with the love I felt for this new creature in my life.”
Kapranos adds that he did not initially write the album with a concept, but instead used a spontaneous approach in which one song influenced the next. However, a common theme soon emerged that threaded through all the songs: one of fear, be it of leaving an institution, leaving a relationship or entering a commitment.
In an exclusive interview with the Post, he recalls: “I was talking to Bob [Hardy, bassist] about this and we realised that even though we all experience these fears, the way we respond to them is individual. That’s how we find our personality and humanity.
“There seemed to be something fascinating about that.”


The band from Glasgow are set to return to Hong Kong on December 7 for the Clockenflap Music and Arts Festival, marking their first performance in the city since 2018.
In the seven years since, Franz Ferdinand experienced a global pandemic, released a compilation album titled Hits to the Head and welcomed a new drummer, Audrey Tait, who replaced Paul Thomson in 2021.
The band have stayed true to their sound since their formation in 2002, which has been described as part of the post-punk revival movement that emerged in the early 2000s in the UK and the US. Part of this consistency comes from a bone-deep authenticity, as the members have always stuck to the sound they like.
Kapranos says it is also inspired by his favourite musicians, such as David Bowie, Nirvana and the Rolling Stones, who were “never ashamed of their identity” but always tried new things and pushed artistic and intellectual boundaries.
“Audacious” from The Human Fear, for example, is classic Franz Ferdinand, but Kapranos restricted himself to using only white keys on the piano, which saw him come up with melodies and melodic ideas that were outside his norm. He likens it to a painter having their right hand tied behind their back.
It was also the band’s first time attempting a “lighter song”.
“If it were 20 or 25 years ago, all the smokers in the audience would have put their lighters in the air. I love that. Now it tends to be an iPhone camera light rather than a cigarette lighter.”

Writing a tune to a somewhat old-school rock ‘n’ roll concept is not the only nod to nostalgia for the band, whose five members still record in Scotland, in a former painter’s studio that Kapranos has owned since the 2000s.
He says the studio is “very Scottish” with a wood fire in the middle of it, which “completely changes the atmosphere. The room itself is maybe the final instrument that I use to express myself with, and what a glorious thing it is.”
During the pandemic lockdown, it became his mission to redesign the acoustics. He bought as many books as he could on the physics of “how sound waves travel within a room, how they respond to different materials and how they diffuse”. He put this new technical knowledge into practice when producing The Human Fear.
He adds, though, that you can never replicate the sound of a particular place with a plug-in. “You’re never going to replicate that with any digital effect that you add to it. That [ambience] is something you can only get from playing together in that room.”
He still believes in the magic of live performances. “I love the fact that no show will be exactly the same, and that [each] performance is unique to that particular location, that particular time and the people there in that place. When it’s a proper live band, you know it’s a distinct performance that you’re only ever going to get once,” he says.

For instance, there was “a good intensity” in Hong Kong when Franz Ferdinand played for the first time in 2006, at the Queen Elizabeth Stadium in Wan Chai.
He fondly remembers a “really, really good” energy at the concerts and “how international the audiences tend to be”, especially at Clockenflap, where the band headlined in 2013.
“That’s the history of Hong Kong, isn’t it? It’s so much China, but so many other places have come to make it Hong Kong.
“I’ve played in mainland China as well, and I loved that; those gigs were fantastic. But the identities of mainland China and Hong Kong are completely different from each other.
“It’s a bit like the difference between Scotland and England. They’re both in the United Kingdom, but they are very, very, very different from each other. There’s a vibrancy to Hong Kong, which I really found in the concerts. It’s unique. I love it because there is nowhere in the world quite like Hong Kong.”

Kapranos compares his impression of Hong Kong, which he calls “one of my favourite places on Earth”, with the feeling of discovering a certain dish in a restaurant that has more flavour than any others, “just like it’s managed to capture everything that has been put inside”.
In a way, with its multidisciplinary art-making, Franz Ferdinand is quite similar. Kapranos finds that most musicians see their work as more than just music, as it also encompasses a literary element in the lyrics and a visual element in areas such as stage dressing and album artwork.
To the band, inspiration can come from visual arts and literature as much as it does from other music that the members listen to, which all contribute to their collective identity and expression.
As with most art forms, there is a technical aspect to music that can be very methodical, something Kapranos has learned since he became active in Glasgow’s music scene in the 1990s. He also enjoys letting his mind roam wild in the early stages of songwriting, creating with a free and unhindered mind and letting the stream of consciousness take hold.
After the out-of-control flow state, he goes back with a scalpel to investigate the “splurge of creation”, which is why he says that taste is essential.
“You learn theory and technique, but what you can’t learn is taste. Taste dictates how you apply your knowledge,” he says.
“You can have somebody who has an incredible technical ability but has terrible taste – what they make is technically perfect but awful. In the same way, you can have somebody who has very little technical ability but has something artistically magical about them, and what they express is wonderful, if crude.”

He cites Hitchcock/Truffaut, a 1966 book based on an interview with English filmmaker Alfred Hitchcock four years earlier by French filmmaker Francois Truffaut. In the interview, Hitchcock divulged that there was a long period of time between him learning what would become his signature in-camera effect – the dolly zoom, also known as the Hitchcock shot – and actually using it.
“He had to wait until he had the moment where it was the perfect device for expressing the emotion that the viewer was supposed to feel,” Kapranos says. “I thought that was amazing and such an important lesson for any artist, no matter what medium in which you work.”
He adds: “The balance [between the technical and the emotional] is what I love about any art form. It’s the humanity and personality that you’re expressing as an artist, but you’re allowing other people to understand the things that we can’t express in words alone.
“The things we can’t explain for ourselves, we understand through art.”

data.title: Children as content? Singaporean ‘sharenting’ videos stir privacy, mental health worries
data.description:
Alex Lee, a Singaporean parent-blogger who has spent years documenting his children’s lives online, recently posted a video of his 12-year-old son opening the results of his Primary School Leaving Examination (PSLE) – a high-stakes test that shapes most pupils’ secondary school paths.
In the clip, which has now drawn more than 3 million views, it cuts from his son, Daken, looking at his scores to him in his father’s arms as Lee asks, “Not as good as you expected?”
Daken agrees as his father tells him, “But you’ve got to try harder in secondary school, OK?” The video then cuts to Lee addressing the camera and sharing some details about Daken’s scores before he asks his son how he feels about his PSLE result. Daken replies he is “a little bit disappointed” and agrees when his father asks if he now understands the importance of doing his homework.
Lee’s clip, posted on Tuesday, has set off a wider debate across Singapore about “sharenting” – the growing trend of parents posting about their children’s lives online – and whether such content might expose children to unwanted scrutiny or emotional harm.
At the heart of the debate is a simple question: where should parents draw the line between documenting family milestones and protecting a child’s privacy?












View this post on Instagram























A post shared by Lee Family 李家出走 (@familee.sg)



Psychologists told This Week in Asia the answer was increasingly fraught amid rising comparison culture, fast-moving digital norms and the permanence of online reputations.
In Lee’s clip, the 40-year-old content creator tells his audience that the moment should be seen in perspective. “Hopefully this little setback will encourage him to work harder in secondary school, no use harping on results any more, he will just have to play with the cards he was dealt,” he tells viewers.
Many praised Lee for being calm and supportive. But others accused him of turning a vulnerable moment into content, warning that the video might erode the boy’s self-esteem or leave him open to teasing.
“Please do not put your child’s entire early life on the internet, not only is it not safe, it is not good for the child’s mental health,” one user commented. Another warned that people who watched the clip could “rub salt into [their son’s] wound” if they met him in person.
Sharing is caring?
Lee told This Week in Asia that he sought his son’s consent before posting the video. “He is totally fine with me posting it even if he did not get a good result, because then, we can show other kids who also did not get a good result that it is not the end of the world,” he said.
Lee’s video did not surface in isolation. In the days surrounding the release of PSLE results, social media feeds were flooded with parents documenting their children opening their folders, celebrating highs or working through lows.
Among them was tattoo artist Catt Tan, who posted a video of results predictions by herself, her husband and her son, before cutting to their reactions when receiving her son’s results slip.
“I post moments that are important for my family, and sharing them helps me record memories while also connecting with others experiencing similar stages in life,” said Tan, 31. “Without these videos, a lot of emotions and memories get lost.”


She said most responses were congratulatory, though a handful questioned the need to publicise exam results. “I personally don’t view PSLE results as something that needs to be hidden unless someone feels ashamed of their child’s results. I’m proud of my son,” she said.
Psychologists and experts said the surge in PSLE-related “sharenting” reflects a broader tension in Singapore’s competitive academic culture, where parents’ desire to model openness and support can be constructive, yet the public nature of such posts exposes children to unsolicited remarks and may fuel comparison culture among families.
Videos that highlight parental support, encouragement and emotional safety regardless of results can help normalise prioritising a child’s well-being over academic performance, said Vivien Yang, a psychologist at Bloom Child Psychology.
Sharing can also help families document meaningful milestones and preserve emotional memories, according to Jung Younbo, an associate professor at Nanyang Technological University’s Wee Kim Wee School of Communication and Information.
“In a world where digital presence and self-presentation matter, such content can even serve as a personal archive or future portfolio for the child,” he added.
Risk to self-esteem
On the flip side, sharenting may expose children to unsolicited remarks from strangers, and risk harming their self-esteem, psychologists say.
They added that the knowledge of potentially having their reactions shared online might stop children from showing their emotions freely, which might hinder healthy emotional development.
Shawn Ee, a clinical psychologist at The Psychology Practice, said sharenting will leave digital footprints that may disadvantage their children in the future. “These personal moments can be used by peers to tease, bully or socially exclude. Some images may also be circulated without context and cause long-term harm to the child’s reputation.”
Jung warned that children could suffer a loss of identity and confidence when they were no longer at the centre of their parent’s social media activity.
“Being treated like a public figure at a young age may lead some children to see themselves as celebrities,” he said. “If attention decreases over time, as often happens on social media, they may experience disappointment, loss of identity, or lowered self-worth, similar to the experiences of idol trainees or young performers who enjoy brief visibility before fading from public attention.”

He suggested limited content access to close friends and trusted individuals depending on the sensitivity of the videos. “Once a video is posted, the content enters a space where reactions are unpredictable and beyond parental control,” he said.
Lee and Tan told This Week in Asia that they set personal guidelines to protect the privacy and well-being of their children.
According to Tan, she does not share her son’s personal details, such as his identification number, and makes an effort to omit or black out images which reveal such information. “I share moments, not information that can compromise safety.”
Lee said he ensured constant communication with his two sons when uploading content. “I will not shame them on purpose,” he said. “Something might be embarrassing now, but it’ll be funny when you look back in a few months.”
By sharing family experiences, he hoped that other children and parents would be able to learn from their videos. “We’ve received messages from other kids that they really enjoy watching our videos and streams, because they do not have a complete or happy family and it is through our videos they feel family love,” he said.
